speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, Survivor is Mrs. Ida Paluch. Interviewer, Anita White. We are in Skokie, Illinois. Mrs. Paluch, you said you found a death certificate of your mother. Please explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The death certificate is in German, signed by the head of the Gestapo in the ghetto. And in that death certificate, it says clearly that my mother committed suicide and what date and where-- the address-- and where we lived, and when she was born-- a lot of information in it. When I saw the death certificate, I decided to go to the place where she died, because I was always bothered. Do I remember or I made it out how she died? And at that time, I was already walking with my brother and my sister-in-law, his wife, and we came to the address where she did commit suicide. And before we went in, I said, Adam, I'm going to describe this, this entrance, the stairway. And I did to the detail. I said the stairway will be to the left, and when we go up, there will be a closet. And then it's all made of wood, and there is a big window. And when we came in the entrance to the stairway was on the left. There was a closet nailed down. And obviously, this was the place that I recognized. It was like going back in time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam looked at it, and I said, what does it remind you? And he looks at me-- nothing. I, I don't remember. I cannot lie. When we went out, I really yelled at him. I said, don't you remember anything? Don't you remember your mother, this woman with the braid here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Him and his wife looked at each other, and Adam said, when I met my wife, she had a braid. She must've looked like my mother. That's what they both remembered. After that, we were really anxious to go and find out people who knew us as children, and we went all over Sosnowiec to our old neighborhood, where we found people. First of all, we did not find the house, because it didn't exist anymore. But the people in the neighborhood remember my father named Paluch, because it's a little funny name, Paluch, a finger. And we found a man and an older woman who remembered that my father made him two suits, and she got also made a suit. And they remembered that it was a corner building where he always sat in the window. And another lady was the la-- the daughter of our landlady.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took a picture with her, and she told us she remembers us as twins. And she even asked my mother, how does she feed the children that they look so good? And my mother said, I feed them with cereal. So getting all those pieces of information was so precious to us, but still we did not find really anything to prove that he was born in Sosnowiec. Weren't you surprised to find a death certificate by the Nazis of a Jew who's committed suicide during the Aktion, during action? Isn't that unusual?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very much surprised. I never, never expected to get it because no one has it. Even the lady, the clerk who gave us, she said, this is the only death certificate that we have. And she said, maybe because it was such a tragedy and so known about it. She must have been one of the first ones or the loudest happening that was at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to take you back yet for one second. You mentioned all your aunts, and you mentioned one of your aunt's daughters, Basia, who went to Brazil.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her mother, and what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basia's mother left Sosnowiec and went to Gdynia where she met a Polish man, a sailor. And she got this child, Basia, with her. And she not-- she was not careful enough to hide her identity. She talked too much that she's Jewish, and she was shot in the street by Gestapo. And Basia stayed at home at the time. And she was taken also by a German family as a maid to Germany, where she survived and later was brought by Sonia to Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how large was actually your extended family? How many aunts and uncles did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that in my mother's family, there were about 13 children. I only know of those four aunts and my mother and two uncles' names. One was Abraham, and the other was the one that we found later in Br-- in Russia, and he came to Brazil. So there were two uncles that I recall, but the other people, I do not. I do not know the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did both uncles survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only one, the one in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you recently with, with the interview that you were asked to go to London and BBC. Would you please tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the first miracle that happened to me, finding my brother, just in the recent few weeks, I got a registered letter from the Washington Holocaust Museum that somebody is requesting to find all the Paluchs in their registry. And since I'm a Paluch, this letter belongs to me. And a lady from BBC was starting a pilot show, and somebody wrote in that the mother name, maiden name is Paluch, and she's looking for the father's family side. And she's looking for a Heskel Paluch. When I write-- I wrote-- read the name Heskel, it rang a bell because my father had an older brother, Heskel. And we found out in the papers, in, in the research that they did that we are the only Paluch with an uncle Heskel. And we were taken, flown in really at their expense to London where we met our cousin, who really looks like me too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said we. Does it mean you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam and I, both of us. We were there three days, three wonderful days, where we met our cousin, her beautiful children, all raised very well. Very nice, very warm family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do they live in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in England, though she was born in Belgium, and during the war, she survived in France, also by a Christian family who hid her. And I believe later she told me she was with her own aunt in South Africa, and now when she came back to London, she met her husband and started a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her father, Heskel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heskel and the mother both died in Auschwitz, and the underground-- the resistance underground did-- was able to take out a few children from Auschwitz and distribute them to Christian families. That's how she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you have a larger family. Now you're saying your brother is here with you. Please tell us what's, what's happening there now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother, when I saw him in Poland, he mentioned to me that life in Poland is not anymore well for Jews, and he begged me not to leave him there. He said, are you going to leave me in this cemetery? And I told, no. I'm not going to ever, ever separate from you. And I took him with me on a visitor's visa, but I really want him to stay because after 53 years of being separated, we deserve to be together. I want him to find a sponsor. I'm sending him now to school. He's learning English. Very badly I want a sponsor that would guarantee for him a job, at least for a short time. What he needs is a permit to work. Then he can get more independent and sponsor his family if they want to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you approached, then, organizations, different organizations to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, I did. The Holocaust Memorial Foundation from here is trying to help me, and the Jewish organization of HIAS is doing the legal work for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there then no doubt in your mind or in Adam's that you are sister and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no doubt, 100% we believe we are the ones who were separated in 1942. We have a lot in common. We are, like they say, twins feeling for each other, feeling even the pain, having the same thoughts, and saying the same sentences at the same time. When he was still in Poland, and I was corresponding with him and calling him, I would sit at work and think, it would be nice if Adam would call me. I'm so sad now. And he would call at that moment like telepathia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what is that story about the little song that came to you about the lullaby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we met, we stayed in my friend's house in Bratslav, where we were invited to a wedding. And Adam-- we had like open doors-- and Adam was talking and talking. And I finally said, Adam, please. We have to rest. Tomorrow is the wedding. And he said to me, OK, I'll rest. I'll let you sleep. And then he and me together started to sing [SINGING 'AAA, KOTKI DWA']

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stood up. We turned on the light. I said, how do you know this song? And he says to me, how do you know this song? I said, I have no idea. But we started the same tune at the same time. And I believe in my heart that probably our mother was singing that to us because it's so appropriate for twins. Would you translate the meaning of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please say it in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: It means, little kitten, sleep, sleep, sleep. They're both gray, and they both will sleep to my voice. And there are two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kotki dwa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kotki dwa.

speaker: interviewer
text: It means two cats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two kittens, and she probably thought of us as two kittens.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask you what are now your dreams for the future for you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dream is that he will get citizenship here eventually, at least resident status. He's trying very hard to learn English, and once he will get, you know, to that point that he can be really independent and have a permit to work, he is a very, very, you know, productive person and has all kind of ideas. He's a smart man and also a survivor. He learned all those skills during his life, and I believe he'll be independent, and during holidays, I can call him, come to my house for the holiday, and he'll do the same. And hopefully, he'll be able to bring his children.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I would like to know. What-- have you met his family? What is your attitude towards his family now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would love for him to bring the children so they continue in a Jewish atmosphere to live as Jews, or whatever they choose.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: His oldest son is 27. His name is Grzegorz. The middle son who looks so much like I and the grandfather is Peter, Piotr. And the little one I use Pawel. He's 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you and Adam planning to go back to Poland to look for Genia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only if she is alive, then we will. Otherwise, I don't think I have a reason to go back to Poland. If Genia is alive, I would go in a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how would you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: If she contacts us, because right now, we're doing all the publicity we can. We're talking in front of television. We're talking in front of groups. We're showing her picture. And if someone knows anything about her, they'll let me know, I hope. You know, by coincidence, I met Adam by an article that came from the other end of United States. Who knows where this information is going to come from? I hope it will.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, in your opinion-- what is your opinion? Is it a miracle from God? Is it an accident, or is it just your perseverance that finally got you and your brother together? What is your feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my perseverance was looked up from the air and said, for all this suffering, I'm going to reward Ida and give her back her brother. I think it is God's blessing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to thank you for inviting us to your home and giving us this very moving, miraculous testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. This picture is shown here is my grandfather, Moshe Wajntraub, and his wife, my grandma, Rivka Wajntraub. And it was probably taken a good few years before the war broke out, and I believe in the city of [? Szczekociny ?] where our parents-- where our mother was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whose parents are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our mother's. This is a picture of our parents with my older sister, Genia. Her Jewish name was Gitla. She must be about seven. She was born 1929, and this picture must be from 1938. My mother, Ester, here born in 1906, and my father, Chaim Leizer Paluch, was born in 1904. This is the picture during the war, where my mother on the right is holding the twins. On the very right is Adam, my twin brother, and I am sitting next to him in the dark sweater, Ida. And over the shoulder of my mother is my sister, Genia who's a little older than us, about 10 years. Next to her is our aunt, Rozia, and her son, Abus, in her arm. And this is the aunt who saved my life because she gave me away in Sosnowiec ghetto to her friend, a Christian man, Mr. Maj.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your aunt Ruja?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own aunt had a very similar death as my mother. She committed suicide on the march from Auschwitz to Germany. She hanged herself. This is my Polish family who saved me during the war. This is Mr. Wilhelm Maj, who picked me up from the Sosnowiec ghetto and died himself as a partisan in 1943. And this is his wife, Jozefa Maj, who took me in as her own child, and she died about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in touch after the war, later on, with your adoptive mother and, and stepsister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always kept in touch with them. When I immigrated to Israel, we were corresponding, and I sent gifts even to her daughter for her wedding and for her-- the birth of her two sons. And I always helped my mother by sending her a little money every month so she could survive because she could not survive on the money paid by the Polish government. And she came here twice, the United States. We brought her here for about half a year, and both times it was like heavenly time spent together. And in my mind, they are my guardian angels. This is the grave commemoration. It was done after the war because still then we could not display somebody murdered by the Gestapo that we're commemorating his death, and even write the truth to how he died. And in this picture, I am here with the round hat, and next to me, that triangle hat, is my Polish sister, and my mother is bending down in the fox fur. And behind me, right behind me, is the grandmother, Mrs. Maj. And there is a little boy and two men standing together. The man on the right is Grandpa Maj and his brother. And the two ladies on the very right are also the widows of the cousins who died together with my Polish father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whose grave? Whose grave is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the grave of the family Maj. There are four graves in there. It's a whole monument. This is the picture of me the day after my father picked me up from the Maj family down in Sosnowiec and displayed in Sosnowiec window for the longest time. Even my grandfather, when he looked for me, he found me through that picture in the window. This is my aunt Sonia, my mother's older sister, and her husband, Meyer. They are here on the Queen Elizabeth boat vacationing in Europe in their 50s. This is the picture of the two of us, my husband and I, my husband, Sam Kersz. And it was taken in the winter of 1978. This is my only daughter, Ester, named after my mother Ester, and her husband, Bruce Bellrose. The picture was taken here in Skokie, and it must be in 1989. Those are my two wonderful grandchildren. The little one is Jonathan Bellrose, middle name Alexander. And the older one, the very first, is Daniel Bellrose and middle name Adam. This is my twin brother, Adam, who I found. This picture in an article that was written up about him in an interview in Warsaw Synagogue of Nozyk. And there I recognized that he looks exactly like our grandfather, and I knew. I knew he was my brother. In this picture, his son, Peter, notice the resemblance he is to mine picture. And he thought that this is his father. And he told to the father, I think she is really your sister because she looks like you. And I look like you, but later, we explained that this is the aunt. This is the most amazing resemblance of Adam's older sis-- son, Grzegorz, made now in his city. And this is my father right after the war about the same age, my father, Paluch. And this is in Bratslav, and now the comparison is so great that me and Adam think that they're looking like real family, that he belongs to our family because he looks like his grandfather. Here on the right is Adam's oldest son, Grzegorz, and in the middle is my father and my sister. And they all look like one family. This is the resemblance that both of us see as special as much as Adam's son and my father. If you put glasses on my father, they'd probably look like brothers or twin brothers. This is a death certificate of my mother, the only one found in Sosnowiec, and on the bottom, it explains how she died. And shamelessly, they wrote that she committed suicide but not the cause of the suicide, that they were the one who caused her death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who issued that particular certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: The commandant of the city of Sosnowiec, and he was the head of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Ida Paluch, would you please introduce the gentleman next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my twin brother who I found not long ago, only three months ago. And his name is Adam Paluch. His real name is Adam Paluch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel now about having your brother living here with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It gives me a sense of security that I too have family of my own, and it fulfills the missing hole in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just in facts?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [POLISH] Ida Paluch, tell us now, in what other intuitive ways do you really know that Adam is your brother? How do you react as twins? Please tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The longer we are together, we say the same things at the same time, the same comments. We like the same foods. We act the same way in certain situations. We love the same music. And when I was waiting for him for the three months that we were supposed to meet, every time I thought about him, I said, wouldn't it be nice if Adam would call me now? He would call. And at a certain point, the two of us decided, since we think alike, let's write it down. And when we wrote down my part of my thought, and he wrote his, we opened up those two, you know, of paper. We were amazed. I screamed because he said the same thing as I.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you have absolutely no doubt that this is your twin brother, Adam Paluch?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't have no doubt that he's my brother. I knew it from the first moment I saw him in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that case, indeed, this is a miraculous thing that after so many years--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a blessing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and a blessing that you found each other. Would you please tell us how active you are and to which organizations you belong because of all that search?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belong to the Holocaust Memorial Foundation. I'm on the directors board. I also am the chairperson of the Holocaust Association of Children Survivors, who are-- survived in the same way I did with some other families or in hiding. And we get together-- we're about the same age-- once a month, and we share our stories. And we understand each other. And that's the environment we feel the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you belong to any other Holocaust organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. Recently, when I visited my brother, I did sign in the Holocaust Association of Warsaw Children Survivors as a, you know, honorary member.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what's close to my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, may we thank you both for letting us into your house and into your heart and giving this absolutely incredible, miraculous story, and we wish you well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And I have to also say that after this miracle, another miracle happened. Both of us found a first cousin. She lives in London, and three weeks ago, we visited her by-- sponsored by the BBC. And we met her for the first time. And amazingly, she looks a lot like I do, and both of us decided that she's really our cousin, because her father's name is just the same name as my father's oldest brother, Heskel Paluch. And this is the niece, Sheldon, with her husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shenton.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shenton. I'm not used yet to the pronunciation-- and her husband Ernest. And this is me, Adam, and Denise.
